---
title: "Quadratic Forms: A Number from a Matrix and a Direction"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# One restrained visual style for every figure in this article, matched to the
# rest of the Geometry-of-Data track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

VEC    = "#2b3a67"   # generic vectors / input directions
HILITE = "#c1121f"   # eigen-axes / principal directions
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"   # second axis / highlights
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PALE   = "#9fb3d1"


def draw_vec(ax, tail, head, color=VEC, lw=2.4, label=None, z=5,
             label_offset=(0.0, 0.0), ls="-"):
    """Draw a 2-D arrow from tail to head with a clean head."""
    ax.annotate("", xy=head, xytext=tail,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0, linestyle=ls), zorder=z)
    if label is not None:
        ax.text(head[0] + label_offset[0], head[1] + label_offset[1], label,
                color=color, fontsize=13, ha="center", va="center", zorder=z + 1)
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This opens **Movement V — Quadratic Forms,
> Positive-Definiteness, and the SVD**. It assumes the **dot product** and the norm
> $\|x\|^2 = x^\top x$ ([GEOM-04]); the **transpose**, its algebra $(AB)^\top = B^\top A^\top$,
> and what it means for a matrix to be **symmetric**, $A = A^\top$ ([GEOM-10]); and — the
> piece it leans on hardest — the **spectral theorem**, which guarantees that a real
> symmetric matrix factors as $A = Q\Lambda Q^\top$ with $Q$ orthogonal ($Q^\top Q = I$) and
> $\Lambda$ diagonal ([GEOM-16]). The one question here: *what is the geometry of the single
> number $q(x) = x^\top A x$, and why does every such surface turn out to be a (rotated,
> scaled) bowl, an upside-down bowl, a saddle, or a degenerate trough — and nothing else?*
> It sets up [GEOM-18] (positive-definite matrices, where the bowl has a unique bottom) and
> [GEOM-25] (the gradient and Hessian of $\tfrac12 x^\top A x - b^\top x$). It is the
> unifying picture behind **variance along a direction**, the **Mahalanobis distance**, the
> **least-squares loss surface**, **Inference's Fisher-information form** ([INFR-B3]), and
> the **quadratic model of a loss** in Optimization.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

A symmetric matrix $A$ turns a direction $x$ into a *single number*, $q(x) = x^\top A x$.
This article is about the shape of that number as $x$ ranges over space. We define the
**quadratic form**, show that only the symmetric part of $A$ matters (so we may always
take $A$ symmetric without loss of generality), and expand $q$ in coordinates so the
reader sees it is exactly a weighted sum of squares and cross-terms. Then we apply the
spectral theorem: rotating to the eigenbasis, $x = Qz$, makes every cross-term vanish and
collapses the form to $q = \sum_i \lambda_i z_i^2$. From that one line the entire geometry
falls out — the **signs of the eigenvalues** decide whether the surface is a bowl (all
$\lambda_i > 0$), an inverted bowl (all $< 0$), a saddle (mixed signs), or a degenerate
ridge/trough (some $\lambda_i = 0$), and the eigenvalues themselves are the curvatures
along the eigen-axes. The single claim this article defends is that **a quadratic form is
nothing but a stretched, rotated paraboloid whose principal axes are the eigenvectors of
$A$ and whose steepnesses are its eigenvalues** — and that this one picture is the same
object reappearing as variance, distance, and loss across the rest of the series.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Feed a symmetric matrix $A$ a direction $x$ and it returns a single scalar,
$$q(x) = x^\top A x = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} A_{ij}\, x_i x_j.$$
This is the **quadratic form** of $A$. It is the only way to build a *degree-two* scalar
out of a vector and a matrix, and it is the multidimensional cousin of $a x^2$ from
high-school algebra — with the eigenvalues of $A$ playing the role of $a$ along each
**eigen-axis**. The spectral theorem ([GEOM-16]) makes this exact: rotate the input into
the eigenbasis, $x = Qz$, and the form becomes a clean weighted sum of squares with **no
cross-terms**,
$$q(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i\, z_i^2.$$
So the surface $q(x)$ is always a paraboloid, tilted to align with the eigenvectors and
stretched by the eigenvalues. The **signs** of the $\lambda_i$ tell you the whole story:
all positive $\Rightarrow$ a bowl with a unique bottom; all negative $\Rightarrow$ an
inverted bowl; mixed $\Rightarrow$ a saddle; any zero $\Rightarrow$ a flat direction. There
is no fifth case.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The expression $x^\top A x$ will look at first like an
arbitrary sandwich of symbols. By the end it should read as a sentence: *take the
direction $x$, measure how far it lies along each eigen-axis, square those measurements,
and add them up weighted by the eigenvalues.* Once you can hear that sentence inside the
matrix product, every quadratic form in the rest of the series — variance, distance,
loss, curvature — becomes the same picture wearing different clothes.

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forced the quadratic form {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **why does the data-science world keep
producing this one expression $x^\top A x$, and what is its shape?** Every algebraic step
below is in service of seeing that shape.

## The shape of "second order" is everywhere

Linear algebra so far has been resolutely *first order*. A matrix map $x \mapsto Ax$ is
linear; the dot product $a^\top x$ is linear in $x$; the gradient of a function is the
best linear approximation. But the quantities we actually care about in data science are
overwhelmingly *second order* — they involve **squares and products of pairs**:

- **Spread.** The variance of a dataset projected onto a direction $u$ is a sum of
  *squared* deviations. As we will derive in @sec-variance, it equals $u^\top \Sigma u$ for
  the covariance matrix $\Sigma$ — a quadratic form.
- **Distance.** The squared length of a vector is $\|x\|^2 = x^\top x$, the quadratic form
  of the identity. The moment we want to measure distance in *correlated* coordinates —
  where some directions are "longer" than others — we get $x^\top M x$ for a general
  symmetric $M$ (the Mahalanobis distance, @sec-mahalanobis).
- **Error.** The least-squares loss $\|y - X\beta\|^2$, expanded, is a quadratic form in
  the parameters $\beta$ plus lower-order terms ([REGR-05], and the full gradient in
  [GEOM-25]). Its *curvature* — what makes it easy or hard to minimize — is captured by a
  symmetric matrix.
- **Curvature itself.** The second-order Taylor expansion of *any* smooth function has a
  quadratic-form term $\tfrac12 \Delta x^\top H \Delta x$, with $H$ the Hessian of second
  derivatives ([GEOM-24]). The local shape of every loss surface in machine learning is a
  quadratic form.

Four different fields, one object. The reason is structural: **the only way to combine a
vector with itself to degree two, linearly in a matrix of coefficients, is
$x^\top A x$.** A degree-one scalar is $a^\top x$; a degree-two scalar is $x^\top A x$;
there is nothing else at second order. So if we want a single, complete understanding of
the *shape* of second-order quantities, we need exactly one thing: the geometry of
$x^\top A x$. That is this article.

## The historical thread: conics, principal axes, and Sylvester

The story is older than data science by two centuries. In the 18th century, mathematicians
classifying the **conic sections** — ellipses, parabolas, hyperbolas — wrote them as
$ax^2 + bxy + cy^2 = \text{const}$. That left-hand side is precisely a two-dimensional
quadratic form. The central problem was: given the messy cross-term $bxy$, *which* conic
is it, and how is it oriented? Euler and Lagrange found that one could always **rotate the
coordinate axes** to kill the cross-term, turning the equation into
$\lambda_1 z_1^2 + \lambda_2 z_2^2 = \text{const}$ — a clean ellipse or hyperbola aligned
with the new axes. Those new axes are the **principal axes**, and the coefficients
$\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ are what we now call eigenvalues.

That "rotate away the cross-term" move *is* the spectral theorem ([GEOM-16]), discovered
geometrically a century before it was stated as a theorem about symmetric matrices. In
1852 James Joseph Sylvester proved that no matter *how* you rotate or rescale the axes, the
**number of positive, negative, and zero coefficients never changes** — his **law of
inertia** (@sec-inertia). That invariance is why "bowl, saddle, or trough" is a genuine
property of the form, not an artifact of the coordinates you happened to use. We inherit
all of this: the quadratic form is the conic-section problem, grown up into $n$ dimensions
and handed the spectral theorem as its solvent.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why restrict to symmetric matrices? Don't quadratic forms make sense for any square
$A$?"* They are *defined* for any square $A$ — $x^\top A x$ is a perfectly good number. But
as we prove in @sec-symmetrize, the value of $x^\top A x$ depends **only on the symmetric
part** $\tfrac12(A + A^\top)$ of $A$; the antisymmetric part contributes exactly zero for
every $x$. So any non-symmetric $A$ produces the *same* form as a unique symmetric matrix,
and we lose nothing — and gain the entire spectral theorem — by agreeing to take $A$
symmetric from the start. An interviewer asking this wants to hear "the antisymmetric part
is invisible to the form, so we symmetrize without loss of generality."
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any algebra, here is the picture to carry. Forget matrices for a moment and recall
the humblest quadratic, $f(t) = a t^2$ from Calc 1. Its graph is a parabola: opening up if
$a > 0$, down if $a < 0$, flat if $a = 0$. The number $a$ is both the *steepness* and,
through its sign, the *shape*.

A quadratic form is this exact idea, but the input is a *direction in space* rather than a
single number $t$. Walk away from the origin along some straight ray — say in direction
$u$ with $\|u\| = 1$ — and watch the value $q(tu)$ as the distance $t$ grows. Because
$q(tu) = (tu)^\top A (tu) = t^2\, u^\top A u$, the value grows as $t^2$: **along every
straight ray through the origin, a quadratic form is an ordinary parabola**, with steepness
$u^\top A u$ depending on which way you set out. Different directions can give different
steepnesses, and even different *signs*.

The whole subject is: *as $u$ sweeps around, how does that steepness $u^\top A u$ change,
and what surface does it trace?* The spectral theorem will tell us there are special
directions — the eigenvectors — along which the parabola is steepest and shallowest, and
that every other direction is a blend of these. @fig-rays shows three rays out of one
2-D form and the parabola you ride along each.

```{python}
#| label: fig-rays
#| fig-cap: 'A quadratic form restricted to a ray is a parabola. **Left:** the level sets (contours) of $q(x) = x^\top A x$ for $A = \begin{psmallmatrix}3 & 1\\ 1 & 2\end{psmallmatrix}$, with three unit directions drawn as arrows. **Right:** the value $q(tu)$ as you walk distance $t$ along each ray. Every ray gives a parabola $q(tu) = t^2\,(u^\top A u)$; the steepness $u^\top A u$ is the only thing that changes from ray to ray. The red direction is the steepest (largest eigenvalue), the green the shallowest.'
A = np.array([[3.0, 1.0], [1.0, 2.0]])

# three unit directions
thetas = [20, 65, 130]
dirs = {f"{th}°": np.array([np.cos(np.radians(th)), np.sin(np.radians(th))])
        for th in thetas}
cols = {f"{thetas[0]}°": HILITE, f"{thetas[1]}°": VEC, f"{thetas[2]}°": ACCENT}

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 3.9))

# left: contours + rays
g = np.linspace(-2.2, 2.2, 220)
GX, GY = np.meshgrid(g, g)
Q = A[0, 0]*GX**2 + 2*A[0, 1]*GX*GY + A[1, 1]*GY**2
cs = axL.contour(GX, GY, Q, levels=[1, 3, 6, 10, 15], colors=GREY, linewidths=1.0)
axL.clabel(cs, inline=True, fontsize=8, fmt="%.0f")
for name, u in dirs.items():
    draw_vec(axL, (0, 0), tuple(1.6*u), color=cols[name], lw=2.4)
axL.set_aspect("equal"); axL.set_xlim(-2.2, 2.2); axL.set_ylim(-2.2, 2.2)
axL.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axL.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
axL.set_title("Level sets and three directions")

# right: parabolas along each ray
ts = np.linspace(-2.0, 2.0, 100)
for name, u in dirs.items():
    steep = u @ A @ u
    axR.plot(ts, ts**2 * steep, color=cols[name], label=f"{name}: $u^\\top\\!Au={steep:.2f}$")
axR.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=1.0)
axR.set_xlabel("distance $t$ along ray"); axR.set_ylabel("$q(tu)=t^2\\,u^\\top\\!Au$")
axR.set_title("Each ray is a parabola"); axR.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

Hold this image. The formal development is just the careful version of "ride a parabola
along each ray, and find the steepest and shallowest rays."

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## Definition, and reading it in coordinates {#sec-definition}

::: {#def-quadform}
**Definition (quadratic form).** Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ real matrix. The **quadratic
form** associated with $A$ is the scalar-valued function
$$
q : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}, \qquad q(x) = x^\top A x .
$$ {#eq-def}
:::

Let us unpack @eq-def so no symbol is mysterious. Here $x = (x_1, \dots, x_n)^\top$ is a
column vector, $A x$ is the usual matrix–vector product (a column vector), and
$x^\top (Ax)$ is a dot product of two column vectors — hence a single number. Writing it
with the matrix entries $A_{ij}$ exposed: the $i$-th entry of $Ax$ is
$(Ax)_i = \sum_{j=1}^n A_{ij} x_j$ (row $i$ of $A$ dotted with $x$), and then $x^\top(Ax)$
dots $x$ against that result:

$$
q(x) = x^\top A x = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i (Ax)_i
      = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i \sum_{j=1}^n A_{ij} x_j
      = \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^n A_{ij}\, x_i x_j .
$$ {#eq-double-sum}

So a quadratic form is a **weighted sum over all ordered pairs $(i,j)$**, with weight
$A_{ij}$ on the product $x_i x_j$. Every term is degree two: either a *square* $x_i^2$
(when $i = j$) or a *cross-term* $x_i x_j$ (when $i \neq j$). This is why it is called
"quadratic" — every term has total degree exactly two, the multidimensional analogue of
$a t^2$.

It pays to see @eq-double-sum fully written out in two dimensions, where you can hold it in
your hand. Take a general $2 \times 2$ matrix and grind the double sum:

$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix},
\qquad x = \begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix}.
$$

The four ordered pairs $(i,j) \in \{(1,1),(1,2),(2,1),(2,2)\}$ contribute
$A_{11}x_1 x_1$, $A_{12}x_1 x_2$, $A_{21}x_2 x_1$, $A_{22}x_2 x_2$. Substituting the
entries:

$$
q(x) = a\,x_1^2 \;+\; b\,x_1 x_2 \;+\; c\,x_2 x_1 \;+\; d\,x_2^2
     \;=\; a\,x_1^2 \;+\; (b + c)\,x_1 x_2 \;+\; d\,x_2^2 .
$$ {#eq-2d-form}

The two off-diagonal entries $b$ and $c$ collapse into a single coefficient $b + c$ on the
cross-term $x_1 x_2$, because $x_1 x_2 = x_2 x_1$. Stare at that: **the form can only feel
the *sum* $b + c$, never $b$ and $c$ separately.** That is the first hint that off-diagonal
asymmetry is invisible to the form — the observation we promote to a theorem next.

## Only the symmetric part matters {#sec-symmetrize}

The collapse $b + c$ generalizes. We can split *any* square matrix into a symmetric piece
and an antisymmetric piece, and show the form ignores the second piece entirely.

::: {#lem-symmetrize}
**Lemma (symmetrization).** For any square matrix $A$ and any vector $x$,
$$
x^\top A x = x^\top A_{\mathrm{sym}}\, x,
\qquad \text{where } A_{\mathrm{sym}} = \tfrac12\bigl(A + A^\top\bigr) = A_{\mathrm{sym}}^\top .
$$
That is, the quadratic form of $A$ equals the quadratic form of its **symmetric part**.
:::

::: {.proof}
First, $x^\top A x$ is a $1 \times 1$ matrix, i.e. a single number, so it equals its own
transpose: $x^\top A x = (x^\top A x)^\top$. Now apply the transpose-of-a-product rule
$(MN)^\top = N^\top M^\top$ ([GEOM-10]) to the right-hand side. Treat the form as the
product of the two factors $M = x^\top$ and $N = A x$, so the rule flips their order:

$$
(x^\top A x)^\top = (x^\top \cdot Ax)^\top = (Ax)^\top (x^\top)^\top .
$$

Apply the same product rule once more to $(Ax)^\top = (A\cdot x)^\top = x^\top A^\top$, and
use $(x^\top)^\top = x$ on the trailing factor:

$$
(Ax)^\top (x^\top)^\top = x^\top A^\top \, x .
$$

So we have established the small but decisive fact
$$
x^\top A x = x^\top A^\top x .
$$ {#eq-transpose-trick}

Both sides are the same number. Average them — add @eq-transpose-trick to the identity
$x^\top A x = x^\top A x$ and divide by two:

$$
x^\top A x = \tfrac12\bigl(x^\top A x + x^\top A^\top x\bigr)
           = x^\top \underbrace{\tfrac12\bigl(A + A^\top\bigr)}_{A_{\mathrm{sym}}} x ,
$$

where the last step factored the common $x^\top(\cdot)x$ out of the sum, legal because
matrix multiplication distributes over addition. Finally $A_{\mathrm{sym}}$ is symmetric:
$A_{\mathrm{sym}}^\top = \tfrac12(A^\top + (A^\top)^\top) = \tfrac12(A^\top + A) =
A_{\mathrm{sym}}$, using $(A^\top)^\top = A$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

In the $2 \times 2$ case this just recovers @eq-2d-form: the symmetric part of
$\begin{psmallmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{psmallmatrix}$ is
$\begin{psmallmatrix} a & (b+c)/2 \\ (b+c)/2 & d \end{psmallmatrix}$, whose form is
$a x_1^2 + 2\cdot\frac{b+c}{2} x_1 x_2 + d x_2^2 = a x_1^2 + (b+c)x_1 x_2 + d x_2^2$,
matching exactly.

::: {.callout-important title="Standing convention from here on"}
Because of @lem-symmetrize, **we lose nothing by assuming $A$ is symmetric**, and we gain
everything: the spectral theorem applies, the eigenvalues are real, and the eigenvectors
are orthogonal. *For the rest of this article $A = A^\top$.* Whenever a quadratic form
arrives wearing a non-symmetric matrix — and they sometimes do, e.g. straight out of an
expansion — silently replace it with $\tfrac12(A + A^\top)$. The form does not change.
:::

A useful convention falls out for the off-diagonal: with $A$ symmetric, $A_{ij} = A_{ji}$,
so each unordered pair $\{i,j\}$ contributes $A_{ij}x_i x_j + A_{ji}x_j x_i = 2A_{ij}x_i
x_j$. The clean coordinate form of a symmetric quadratic form is therefore

$$
q(x) = \sum_{i=1}^n A_{ii}\, x_i^2 \;+\; 2\!\!\sum_{1 \le i < j \le n}\!\! A_{ij}\, x_i x_j ,
$$ {#eq-sym-form}

the diagonal entries weight the pure squares, and *twice* each off-diagonal entry weights
the corresponding cross-term. This factor of two is the source of endless sign-and-half
bookkeeping errors later; pin it down now.

## Diagonalizing the form: the cross-terms vanish {#sec-diagonalize}

The cross-terms in @eq-sym-form are what make a quadratic form hard to read — they tangle
the coordinates together. The central act of this article is to *make them disappear* by
changing to the right coordinate system. The spectral theorem hands us that system for
free.

Recall the spectral theorem ([GEOM-16]): a real symmetric matrix $A$ can be written

$$
A = Q \Lambda Q^\top, \qquad
Q^\top Q = I, \qquad
\Lambda = \operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_n) ,
$$ {#eq-spectral}

where the columns $q_1, \dots, q_n$ of $Q$ are an **orthonormal** set of eigenvectors of
$A$ (so $A q_i = \lambda_i q_i$ and $q_i^\top q_j = \delta_{ij}$), the $\lambda_i$ are the
**real** eigenvalues, and $Q$ being orthogonal means its transpose is its inverse,
$Q^{-1} = Q^\top$. We imported this from [GEOM-16], where both halves — real eigenvalues
and orthogonal eigenvectors — are proved from scratch; here we put it to work.

Substitute @eq-spectral into the form and watch what happens:

$$
q(x) = x^\top A x = x^\top \bigl(Q \Lambda Q^\top\bigr) x
     = \bigl(Q^\top x\bigr)^\top \Lambda \bigl(Q^\top x\bigr) .
$$ {#eq-substituted}

The last equality regrouped the product using associativity and $(Q^\top x)^\top = x^\top
(Q^\top)^\top = x^\top Q$, which is exactly the $x^\top Q$ on the left. Now **define new
coordinates**

$$
z = Q^\top x \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad x = Q z ,
$$ {#eq-change-coords}

the equivalence holding because $Q^\top = Q^{-1}$, so multiplying $z = Q^\top x$ by $Q$
gives $Qz = QQ^\top x = x$. Geometrically $z$ is just $x$ *re-expressed in the eigenbasis*:
$z_i = q_i^\top x$ is the coordinate of $x$ along the $i$-th eigenvector — the amount of
$x$ that points in the $q_i$ direction (this is the "coordinates in an orthonormal basis
are dot products" fact from [GEOM-05]). With this substitution @eq-substituted becomes

$$
q(x) = z^\top \Lambda z .
$$ {#eq-diag-form}

But $\Lambda$ is *diagonal*, so its quadratic form has **no cross-terms at all**. Expanding
$z^\top \Lambda z$ with @eq-double-sum and using $\Lambda_{ij} = 0$ for $i \neq j$ and
$\Lambda_{ii} = \lambda_i$:

$$
z^\top \Lambda z = \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^n \Lambda_{ij}\, z_i z_j
                 = \sum_{i=1}^n \Lambda_{ii}\, z_i^2
                 = \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\, z_i^2 .
$$

We have arrived at the central formula of the article.

::: {#thm-principal-axes}
**Theorem (principal-axis form).** Let $A$ be a real symmetric $n \times n$ matrix with
spectral decomposition $A = Q\Lambda Q^\top$, eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_n$, and
orthonormal eigenvectors $q_1, \dots, q_n$. In the eigen-coordinates $z = Q^\top x$, the
quadratic form is a pure weighted sum of squares:
$$
q(x) = x^\top A x = \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\, z_i^2,
\qquad z_i = q_i^\top x .
$$ {#eq-principal}
:::

::: {.proof}
The chain @eq-substituted → @eq-change-coords → @eq-diag-form → the diagonal expansion is
the proof; every step is an equality, no step assumes anything beyond the spectral
decomposition @eq-spectral (imported and proved in [GEOM-16]) and the transpose algebra of
[GEOM-10]. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the conic-section move of @sec-why made rigorous in $n$ dimensions: **rotate into
the eigenbasis and the cross-terms vanish, leaving one independent parabola per axis.**
Along the $i$-th eigen-axis the form behaves like $\lambda_i z_i^2$ — a parabola of
steepness $\lambda_i$ — and the surface is the sum of these $n$ independent parabolas. The
eigenvectors are the **principal axes**; the eigenvalues are the **curvatures** along them.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What does the change of variables $z = Q^\top x$ do, geometrically?"* It is a pure
**rotation** (and possibly reflection) of the coordinate axes onto the eigenvectors —
because $Q$ is orthogonal, it preserves all lengths and angles ($\|Qz\| = \|z\|$, since
$\|Qz\|^2 = z^\top Q^\top Q z = z^\top z$). So we are not distorting the surface, only
**looking at it from the natural viewpoint** where its axes line up with the coordinate
axes. The value $q$ at each point is unchanged; we have just relabeled the floor. This is
why eigen-coordinates are the right coordinates: they are a rigid re-orientation that
happens to untangle the form.
:::

## The four shapes — and why there is no fifth {#sec-shapes}

Formula @eq-principal makes the classification immediate. The form is a sum of terms
$\lambda_i z_i^2$. Each $z_i^2 \ge 0$ always, so the *sign* of each term is the sign of its
$\lambda_i$. Sweeping over all directions $x$ (equivalently all $z$), the possible
behaviors are exactly determined by the signs of the eigenvalues:

::: {#thm-classification}
**Theorem (classification by eigenvalue signs).** With $q(x) = \sum_i \lambda_i z_i^2$ as
in @eq-principal:

1. **All $\lambda_i > 0$** $\Rightarrow$ $q(x) > 0$ for every $x \neq 0$: a **bowl**
   (elliptic paraboloid) opening upward, with a unique minimum at $x = 0$. The form is
   **positive definite**.
2. **All $\lambda_i < 0$** $\Rightarrow$ $q(x) < 0$ for every $x \neq 0$: an **inverted
   bowl**, unique maximum at $0$. **Negative definite**.
3. **Mixed signs** (some $\lambda_i > 0$, some $< 0$) $\Rightarrow$ $q$ takes both positive
   and negative values: a **saddle**. **Indefinite**.
4. **Some $\lambda_i = 0$, the rest one sign** $\Rightarrow$ $q$ is flat along the
   zero-eigenvalue directions: a **trough / ridge**. **Positive (or negative)
   semidefinite**.
:::

::: {.proof}
*Case 1.* If every $\lambda_i > 0$ and $x \neq 0$, then $z = Q^\top x \neq 0$ (because
$Q^\top$ is invertible, it sends nonzero to nonzero), so at least one $z_k \neq 0$, giving
$z_k^2 > 0$. Then $q(x) = \sum_i \lambda_i z_i^2 \ge \lambda_k z_k^2 > 0$, since every term
is $\ge 0$ and the $k$-th is strictly positive. So $q > 0$ off the origin and $q(0) = 0$ is
the unique minimum.

*Case 2* is Case 1 applied to $-A$ (eigenvalues $-\lambda_i > 0$), flipping every sign.

*Case 3.* Pick an eigenvector $q_p$ with $\lambda_p > 0$ and one $q_m$ with $\lambda_m <
0$. Evaluate the form along each. In eigen-coordinates, $x = q_p$ has $z = e_p$ (the $p$-th
standard basis vector, since $q_i^\top q_p = \delta_{ip}$), so $q(q_p) = \lambda_p > 0$.
Likewise $q(q_m) = \lambda_m < 0$. The form is positive in one direction and negative in
another — a saddle.

*Case 4.* If $\lambda_k = 0$, then moving along $q_k$ leaves the form unchanged:
$q(t q_k) = \lambda_k t^2 = 0$ for all $t$. So $q$ is constant (flat) along that whole
direction, while the nonzero eigenvalues curve it up (or down) in the other directions —
a trough (if the rest are positive) or a ridge (if negative).

These four cases — all-positive, all-negative, mixed, and some-zero — exhaust the
sign-patterns of a finite list of real numbers, so there is no fifth case.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This classification is the deep reason the Core box can promise "bowl, inverted bowl,
saddle, or trough, and nothing else." The next article, [GEOM-18], takes Case 1 — the
positive-definite "good" matrices — and studies it in full, because a bowl with a unique
bottom is exactly the surface optimization and statistics most want. @fig-four-shapes draws
all four.

```{python}
#| label: fig-four-shapes
#| fig-cap: 'The complete zoo of quadratic-form surfaces $q(x)=x^\top A x$ over $\mathbb{R}^2$, one per eigenvalue sign-pattern. **(a)** both eigenvalues positive: a bowl (positive definite). **(b)** both negative: an inverted bowl (negative definite). **(c)** mixed signs: a saddle (indefinite). **(d)** one eigenvalue zero, one positive: a trough, flat along the zero-direction (positive semidefinite). There is no fifth shape — @thm-classification proves these exhaust the cases.'
g = np.linspace(-2, 2, 60)
GX, GY = np.meshgrid(g, g)

forms = [
    (np.array([[2.0, 0.5], [0.5, 1.5]]), "(a) bowl — positive definite\n$\\lambda>0,\\ \\lambda>0$"),
    (np.array([[-2.0, 0.5], [0.5, -1.5]]), "(b) inverted bowl — neg. definite\n$\\lambda<0,\\ \\lambda<0$"),
    (np.array([[1.5, 0.0], [0.0, -1.5]]), "(c) saddle — indefinite\n$\\lambda>0,\\ \\lambda<0$"),
    (np.array([[1.0, 0.0], [0.0, 0.0]]), "(d) trough — pos. semidefinite\n$\\lambda>0,\\ \\lambda=0$"),
]

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(9.2, 7.4))
for k, (M, title) in enumerate(forms, start=1):
    ax = fig.add_subplot(2, 2, k, projection="3d")
    Z = M[0, 0]*GX**2 + 2*M[0, 1]*GX*GY + M[1, 1]*GY**2
    ax.plot_surface(GX, GY, Z, cmap="viridis", linewidth=0, antialiased=True, alpha=0.9)
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$", labelpad=-6); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$", labelpad=-6)
    ax.tick_params(labelsize=7, pad=-2)
    ax.view_init(elev=26, azim=-122)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Level sets: ellipses, hyperbolas, and the eigen-axes you can see {#sec-level-sets}

The 3-D surfaces are vivid but a flatter view is more useful day to day: the **level sets**
$\{x : q(x) = c\}$, the curves where the form equals a constant. In two dimensions these
are the conic sections of @sec-why, and they show the eigenstructure directly.

Set @eq-principal equal to a positive constant $c$ in a positive-definite case, in
eigen-coordinates:

$$
\lambda_1 z_1^2 + \lambda_2 z_2^2 = c
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\frac{z_1^2}{(c/\lambda_1)} + \frac{z_2^2}{(c/\lambda_2)} = 1 ,
$$ {#eq-ellipse}

dividing through by $c$. This is the standard equation of an **ellipse** with semi-axis
lengths $\sqrt{c/\lambda_1}$ along $q_1$ and $\sqrt{c/\lambda_2}$ along $q_2$. Read the
geometry off it: the ellipse's axes point along the eigenvectors, and the semi-axis length
in each direction is $\sqrt{c/\lambda_i}$ — **inversely** proportional to $\sqrt{\lambda_i}$.
A *large* eigenvalue means the form climbs fast, so it hits the level $c$ quickly: a
**short** axis. A *small* eigenvalue means a slow climb and a **long** axis. The ellipse is
squashed along the steep (large-eigenvalue) directions and stretched along the shallow
ones.

When the eigenvalues have opposite signs (the saddle), @eq-ellipse becomes a difference of
squares equal to a constant — the equation of a **hyperbola** — and the level sets open out
along the directions where the form is negative. @fig-level-sets shows both, with the
eigenvectors drawn so you can confirm they are the axes.

```{python}
#| label: fig-level-sets
#| fig-cap: 'Level sets carry the eigenstructure on their face. **Left:** a positive-definite form; the contours are concentric ellipses whose axes are the eigenvectors $q_1,q_2$ (red, green) and whose semi-axis lengths go as $1/\sqrt{\lambda_i}$ — short along the steep large-$\lambda$ direction, long along the shallow one. **Right:** an indefinite form; the contours are hyperbolas opening along the negative-eigenvalue direction. The eigenvectors are the symmetry axes in both cases.'
def eig_contours(ax, A, title, levels):
    g = np.linspace(-3, 3, 300)
    GX, GY = np.meshgrid(g, g)
    Q = A[0, 0]*GX**2 + 2*A[0, 1]*GX*GY + A[1, 1]*GY**2
    cs = ax.contour(GX, GY, Q, levels=levels, colors=GREY, linewidths=1.1)
    ax.clabel(cs, inline=True, fontsize=8, fmt="%.0f")
    w, V = np.linalg.eigh(A)
    for k, col in zip(range(2), (HILITE, ACCENT)):
        v = V[:, k]
        draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), tuple(1.6*v), color=col, lw=2.6,
                 label=f"$q_{k+1}\\ (\\lambda={w[k]:.1f})$",
                 label_offset=(0.35*np.sign(v[0]+1e-9), 0.30*np.sign(v[1]+1e-9)))
    ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xlim(-3, 3); ax.set_ylim(-3, 3)
    ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$"); ax.set_title(title)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 4.6))
eig_contours(axL, np.array([[3.0, 1.2], [1.2, 1.0]]),
             "Positive definite: ellipses", [1, 3, 6, 10])
eig_contours(axR, np.array([[1.0, 1.6], [1.6, -1.0]]),
             "Indefinite: hyperbolas", [-6, -2, 2, 6])
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"If the level sets of a positive-definite form are ellipses with axes along the
eigenvectors, what does a near-singular matrix (one tiny eigenvalue) look like?"* A
**hugely elongated** ellipse — a long thin cigar — because the semi-axis $\sqrt{c/\lambda}$
blows up as $\lambda \to 0^+$. The form barely changes along the small-eigenvalue
direction. This is the geometric face of an **ill-conditioned** matrix: the ratio of
longest to shortest axis is $\sqrt{\lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}}$, and that ratio is the
condition number's fingerprint ([GEOM-26]). It is also exactly what near-collinear
predictors do to the least-squares loss surface ([REGR-07]) — a valley so stretched that
the minimum is poorly pinned down in one direction.
:::

## The extremes: Rayleigh quotient and the steepest directions {#sec-rayleigh}

We promised that the eigenvectors are the steepest and shallowest directions. Here is the
precise statement, and it is one of the most useful facts in applied linear algebra. On the
unit sphere $\|u\| = 1$, the form's value $u^\top A u$ is exactly the steepness of the
parabola along direction $u$ (from @sec-intuition). How big and small can it get?

::: {#thm-rayleigh}
**Theorem (Rayleigh bounds).** For a symmetric $A$ with eigenvalues
$\lambda_{\min} = \lambda_1 \le \dots \le \lambda_n = \lambda_{\max}$, and any unit vector
$u$ ($\|u\| = 1$),
$$
\lambda_{\min} \;\le\; u^\top A u \;\le\; \lambda_{\max},
$$
with equality at the bottom when $u$ is an eigenvector for $\lambda_{\min}$, and at the top
when $u$ is an eigenvector for $\lambda_{\max}$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Write $u$ in eigen-coordinates, $z = Q^\top u$. Because $Q$ is orthogonal it preserves
length, so $\|z\|^2 = z^\top z = u^\top Q Q^\top u = u^\top u = 1$ — the new coordinates
also lie on the unit sphere, $\sum_i z_i^2 = 1$. By @eq-principal,

$$
u^\top A u = \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i z_i^2 .
$$

This is a **weighted average** of the eigenvalues: the weights $z_i^2$ are nonnegative and
sum to one. An average of numbers can be no larger than the largest of them and no smaller
than the smallest:

$$
\lambda_{\min} = \lambda_{\min}\sum_i z_i^2 \le \sum_i \lambda_i z_i^2
              \le \lambda_{\max}\sum_i z_i^2 = \lambda_{\max} ,
$$

where the middle inequalities replaced each $\lambda_i$ by the smallest (resp. largest)
eigenvalue term by term, legal because the weights $z_i^2 \ge 0$. The upper bound is
*achieved* by putting all the weight on the top eigenvalue: choose $u = q_n$, so $z = e_n$,
$z_n^2 = 1$, and $u^\top A u = \lambda_n = \lambda_{\max}$. Likewise $u = q_1$ achieves
$\lambda_{\min}$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The ratio $R(x) = \dfrac{x^\top A x}{x^\top x}$ for any $x \neq 0$ is called the **Rayleigh
quotient**; dividing by $x^\top x = \|x\|^2$ is exactly the normalization to a unit vector,
so @thm-rayleigh says the Rayleigh quotient ranges over $[\lambda_{\min}, \lambda_{\max}]$,
hitting the ends at the extreme eigenvectors. This is the entire engine of **principal
component analysis** — "find the direction of maximum variance" *is* "maximize the Rayleigh
quotient of the covariance matrix," whose answer is the top eigenvector — though the
statistical packaging belongs to Unsupervised Learning, which links back here for the
algebra. @fig-rayleigh shows the steepness sweeping between the two eigenvalues as the
direction rotates.

```{python}
#| label: fig-rayleigh
#| fig-cap: 'The Rayleigh quotient $u^\top A u$ as the unit direction $u=(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)$ rotates, for $A=\begin{psmallmatrix}3&1\\1&2\end{psmallmatrix}$. It oscillates strictly between $\lambda_{\min}$ (dashed) and $\lambda_{\max}$ (dotted), touching each exactly when $u$ aligns with the corresponding eigenvector (vertical markers). Every achievable steepness of the form lives in this band.'
A = np.array([[3.0, 1.0], [1.0, 2.0]])
w, V = np.linalg.eigh(A)              # ascending eigenvalues
th = np.linspace(0, np.pi, 400)
U = np.vstack([np.cos(th), np.sin(th)])
R = np.einsum("ij,ji->i", U.T @ A, U)  # u^T A u for each column

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.0))
ax.plot(np.degrees(th), R, color=VEC, label="$u^\\top A u$")
ax.axhline(w[1], color=HILITE, ls=":", lw=1.8, label=f"$\\lambda_\\max={w[1]:.3f}$")
ax.axhline(w[0], color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.8, label=f"$\\lambda_\\min={w[0]:.3f}$")
# angles of eigenvectors
for k, col in zip(range(2), (ACCENT, HILITE)):
    ang = np.degrees(np.arctan2(V[1, k], V[0, k])) % 180
    ax.axvline(ang, color=col, ls=":", lw=1.0)
ax.set_xlabel("direction angle $\\theta$ (degrees)")
ax.set_ylabel("steepness $u^\\top A u$")
ax.set_title("Steepness is bounded by the eigenvalues")
ax.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9, loc="center right")
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Sylvester's law of inertia: the signs are intrinsic {#sec-inertia}

A nagging worry: we read the four shapes off the *eigenvalues*, which came from the
*orthogonal* change of coordinates $z = Q^\top x$. What if we had used some *other* change
of coordinates — a non-orthogonal one — and gotten different signs? Could "bowl vs. saddle"
depend on the coordinate system? Sylvester's 1852 theorem says no, and it is what makes the
classification a real property of the form.

Define the **inertia** of a symmetric matrix as the triple $(n_+, n_-, n_0)$: the number of
positive, negative, and zero eigenvalues. (These sum to $n$.)

::: {#thm-inertia}
**Theorem (Sylvester's law of inertia).** If $A = C^\top D C$ for *any* invertible matrix
$C$ and diagonal $D$, then $D$ has the same number of positive, negative, and zero diagonal
entries as $A$ has positive, negative, and zero eigenvalues. In short: **the inertia
$(n_+, n_-, n_0)$ is invariant under any invertible change of variables**, not just
orthogonal ones.
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove the part that matters for the classification — that $n_+$, the dimension of the
largest subspace on which $q$ is strictly positive, is well defined regardless of how we
diagonalize. Call a subspace $S$ **positive** for $q$ if $q(x) > 0$ for every nonzero
$x \in S$. Let $p$ be the maximum possible dimension of a positive subspace; this number is
defined purely from $q$ — it mentions no coordinate system.

From the spectral decomposition, the span of the eigenvectors with positive eigenvalues is
a positive subspace of dimension $n_+$ (on it, $q(x) = \sum_{\lambda_i > 0}\lambda_i z_i^2 >
0$ for $x \neq 0$). So $p \ge n_+$. Conversely, suppose $S$ is a positive subspace with
$\dim S > n_+$. Let $W$ be the span of the eigenvectors with eigenvalue $\le 0$, which has
dimension $n_- + n_0 = n - n_+$. Since $\dim S + \dim W > n_+ + (n - n_+) = n$, the two
subspaces must intersect in some nonzero vector $v$ (two subspaces whose dimensions sum to
more than $n$ cannot meet only at the origin — a counting fact from the dimension formula,
[GEOM-03]). But $v \in W$ forces $q(v) = \sum_{\lambda_i \le 0}\lambda_i z_i^2 \le 0$, while
$v \in S$ forces $q(v) > 0$ — a contradiction. Hence no positive subspace exceeds dimension
$n_+$, so $p = n_+$.

Now take *any* diagonalization $A = C^\top D C$ with $C$ invertible and $D =
\operatorname{diag}(d_1, \dots, d_n)$, and let $d_+$ be its number of strictly positive
entries. We show $d_+ = p$, which (since $p = n_+$) forces $d_+ = n_+$ for every such
factorization — the invariance we want. Substituting and using $(Cx)^\top = x^\top C^\top$,
the form reads, in the coordinates $y = Cx$,
$$
q(x) = x^\top C^\top D C x = (Cx)^\top D (Cx) = y^\top D y = \sum_{j=1}^n d_j\, y_j^2 .
$$
Because $C$ is invertible, $y = Cx$ is a bijection between $x$-space and $y$-space, so we
may build subspaces in $y$-coordinates and pull them back.

*$d_+ \le p$.* Let $P$ be the set of $x$ whose image $y = Cx$ is supported on the positive
entries of $D$ — that is, $y_j = 0$ whenever $d_j \le 0$. This is a subspace of dimension
$d_+$ (it is the preimage under the invertible map $C$ of the $d_+$-dimensional coordinate
subspace spanned by the positive-$d_j$ axes, and an invertible map preserves dimension,
[GEOM-03]). On $P$, any nonzero $x$ has nonzero $y$ with all weight on positive $d_j$, so
$q(x) = \sum_{d_j > 0} d_j\, y_j^2 > 0$. Thus $P$ is a positive subspace of dimension $d_+$,
giving $d_+ \le p$.

*$d_+ \ge p$.* Conversely let $N$ be the preimage of the axes with $d_j \le 0$ (so $y_j = 0$
whenever $d_j > 0$); it has dimension $n - d_+$, and on it $q(x) = \sum_{d_j \le 0} d_j\,
y_j^2 \le 0$. If some positive subspace $S$ had $\dim S = p > d_+$, then $\dim S + \dim N >
d_+ + (n - d_+) = n$, so $S$ and $N$ meet in a nonzero $v$ (same counting fact, [GEOM-03]);
but $v \in N$ gives $q(v) \le 0$ and $v \in S$ gives $q(v) > 0$, a contradiction. Hence
$p \le d_+$.

Together $d_+ = p = n_+$: every diagonalization of $A$, orthogonal or not, has exactly
$n_+$ positive diagonal entries. The identical argument applied to $-q$ (whose positive
subspaces are $q$'s negative subspaces) pins down $n_-$ as the invariant count of negative
entries, and $n_0 = n - n_+ - n_-$ follows. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The payoff is conceptual but important: **"this form is a saddle" is a fact about the form,
not about your basis.** You may diagonalize by the orthogonal spectral route (which also
gives the right *curvatures*), or by completing the square (a non-orthogonal route that is
faster by hand but distorts the axes); either way you get the same count of $+$, $-$, $0$
signs, hence the same shape verdict. The eigenvalues give *more* — the actual steepnesses
and the true principal axes — but the **signature** $(n_+, n_-, n_0)$ alone already decides
bowl/saddle/trough.

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run the machinery on three honest examples: variance along a direction (where the
form is born from data), the Mahalanobis distance (where the form *is* a notion of
distance), and a deliberately degenerate case at the edge of applicability.

## Example 1 — variance along a direction: $u^\top \Sigma u$ {#sec-variance}

This is the example that makes quadratic forms matter for data science, so we build it from
the definition. Suppose we have $m$ data points $x^{(1)}, \dots, x^{(m)}$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$,
already **centered** so their mean is zero ($\frac1m\sum_k x^{(k)} = 0$). Pick a direction
$u$ with $\|u\| = 1$ and ask: *how spread out is the data when projected onto $u$?* The
projection of point $x^{(k)}$ onto the unit direction $u$ is the scalar $u^\top x^{(k)}$
(the signed length of the shadow, [GEOM-06]). The spread is the average squared projection
(the variance, since the projections have mean zero):

$$
\operatorname{Var}_u = \frac1m \sum_{k=1}^m \bigl(u^\top x^{(k)}\bigr)^2 .
$$

Now turn the scalar square into a quadratic form. A scalar equals its own transpose, so
$(u^\top x^{(k)})^2 = (u^\top x^{(k)})(u^\top x^{(k)}) = (u^\top x^{(k)})({x^{(k)}}^\top u)$,
and the middle two factors combine into the outer product ${x^{(k)}}{x^{(k)}}^\top$:

$$
\operatorname{Var}_u
= \frac1m \sum_{k=1}^m u^\top x^{(k)} {x^{(k)}}^\top u
= u^\top \underbrace{\Bigl(\frac1m \sum_{k=1}^m x^{(k)} {x^{(k)}}^\top\Bigr)}_{\textstyle \Sigma} u
= u^\top \Sigma u ,
$$ {#eq-variance-form}

where we factored the constant $u^\top(\cdot)u$ out of the sum. The matrix $\Sigma =
\frac1m \sum_k x^{(k)}{x^{(k)}}^\top$ is the **covariance matrix** of the centered data; it
is symmetric (each outer product $x x^\top$ is symmetric, and a sum of symmetric matrices
is symmetric), so everything above applies. *Variance along a direction is a quadratic
form.* The interpretation of $\Sigma$ as a statistical object is Probability's to own; here
it is simply a symmetric matrix whose quadratic form we now read geometrically.

By @thm-rayleigh, the direction of **maximum variance** is the top eigenvector of $\Sigma$
(the principal component), and the variance there is $\lambda_{\max}$; the minimum-variance
direction is the bottom eigenvector. The ellipse of @eq-ellipse is literally the **scatter
shape** of the cloud. @fig-variance makes this concrete on a real-looking 2-D cloud.

```{python}
#| label: fig-variance
#| fig-cap: 'Variance along a direction is the quadratic form $u^\top\Sigma u$. **Left:** a centered data cloud with its covariance ellipse (the level set $u^\top\Sigma^{-1}u=$const that the data trace out); the eigenvectors of $\Sigma$ (red, green) are the directions of maximum and minimum variance, scaled by $\sqrt{\lambda}$. **Right:** the projected variance $u^\top\Sigma u$ as the direction rotates, peaking at the top eigenvector exactly as @thm-rayleigh predicts. The eigenvalues are the variances along the principal axes.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
# a correlated cloud
true_cov = np.array([[4.0, 2.2], [2.2, 2.0]])
L = np.linalg.cholesky(true_cov)
data = (L @ rng.normal(size=(2, 400))).T
data -= data.mean(axis=0)
Sigma = (data.T @ data) / data.shape[0]
w, V = np.linalg.eigh(Sigma)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 4.2))
axL.scatter(data[:, 0], data[:, 1], s=10, color=PALE, alpha=0.7, zorder=1)
# covariance ellipse at 2 std
tt = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 200)
ell = (V @ np.diag(2*np.sqrt(w)) @ np.vstack([np.cos(tt), np.sin(tt)]))
axL.plot(ell[0], ell[1], color=GREY, lw=1.5, zorder=2)
for k, col in zip(range(2), (ACCENT, HILITE)):
    draw_vec(axL, (0, 0), tuple(2*np.sqrt(w[k])*V[:, k]), color=col, lw=2.6,
             label=f"$\\lambda={w[k]:.2f}$",
             label_offset=(0.5*np.sign(V[0, k]+1e-9), 0.4*np.sign(V[1, k]+1e-9)))
axL.set_aspect("equal"); axL.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axL.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
axL.set_title("Cloud, covariance ellipse, principal axes")

th = np.linspace(0, np.pi, 300)
U = np.vstack([np.cos(th), np.sin(th)])
var_u = np.einsum("ij,ji->i", U.T @ Sigma, U)
axR.plot(np.degrees(th), var_u, color=VEC)
axR.axhline(w[1], color=HILITE, ls=":", lw=1.6, label=f"max var $={w[1]:.2f}$")
axR.axhline(w[0], color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.6, label=f"min var $={w[0]:.2f}$")
axR.set_xlabel("direction angle (degrees)"); axR.set_ylabel("$u^\\top\\Sigma u$ (variance)")
axR.set_title("Projected variance vs. direction"); axR.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Example 2 — the Mahalanobis distance: a form that measures distance {#sec-mahalanobis}

The ordinary squared distance from the origin, $\|x\|^2 = x^\top I x$, is the quadratic form
of the identity — all eigenvalues equal to one, so the level sets are *circles* and every
direction counts equally. But in correlated data this is the wrong distance. A point two
standard deviations out along a high-variance direction is unremarkable; the same Euclidean
distance along a low-variance direction is a genuine outlier. We want a distance that
**accounts for the spread of the data** — that measures "how many standard deviations out"
in each direction.

That distance is the quadratic form of the *inverse* covariance matrix:

$$
d_M(x)^2 = x^\top \Sigma^{-1} x .
$$ {#eq-mahalanobis}

Why the inverse? Diagonalize: with $\Sigma = Q\Lambda Q^\top$, the inverse is $\Sigma^{-1} =
Q\Lambda^{-1}Q^\top$ (orthogonal $Q$ makes the inverse trivial — invert the eigenvalues,
keep the eigenvectors; from [GEOM-16]). So in eigen-coordinates $z = Q^\top x$,

$$
d_M(x)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^n \frac{z_i^2}{\lambda_i} .
$$ {#eq-maha-diag}

Each coordinate's contribution is **divided by its variance** $\lambda_i$. A step along a
high-variance axis (large $\lambda_i$) costs little Mahalanobis distance; a step along a
low-variance axis costs a lot. This is precisely "how many standard deviations": the level
set $d_M = 1$ is the covariance ellipse itself — large $\lambda_i$ gives a long axis
$\sqrt{\lambda_i}$ (by @eq-ellipse with the *inverse* eigenvalues), so the unit-Mahalanobis
contour hugs the natural shape of the cloud. The eigenvalues being **positive** (a
covariance matrix is positive semidefinite, and positive definite when the data are not
trapped in a lower-dimensional subspace — [GEOM-18]) is exactly what makes $d_M^2 \ge 0$ a
sensible squared distance. @fig-mahalanobis contrasts Euclidean and Mahalanobis level sets
on the same cloud.

```{python}
#| label: fig-mahalanobis
#| fig-cap: 'Euclidean vs. Mahalanobis distance, same cloud. **Grey circles** are Euclidean level sets $x^\top x=$const — they treat every direction equally and so flag the wrong points as far. **Red ellipses** are Mahalanobis level sets $x^\top\Sigma^{-1}x=$const — stretched to match the data''s spread, so ''far'' means ''far in standard-deviation units.'' The starred point is only moderately far Euclidean-wise but very far in Mahalanobis terms, because it lies off the cloud''s long axis.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
true_cov = np.array([[5.0, 3.0], [3.0, 2.2]])
L = np.linalg.cholesky(true_cov)
data = (L @ rng.normal(size=(2, 350))).T
data -= data.mean(axis=0)
Sigma = (data.T @ data) / data.shape[0]
Sinv = np.linalg.inv(Sigma)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 5.4))
ax.scatter(data[:, 0], data[:, 1], s=10, color=PALE, alpha=0.7, zorder=1)
gg = np.linspace(-9, 9, 400)
GX, GY = np.meshgrid(gg, gg)
Euc = GX**2 + GY**2
Mah = Sinv[0, 0]*GX**2 + 2*Sinv[0, 1]*GX*GY + Sinv[1, 1]*GY**2
ax.contour(GX, GY, Euc, levels=[4, 16, 36], colors=GREY, linewidths=1.2, linestyles="--")
ax.contour(GX, GY, Mah, levels=[1, 4, 9], colors=HILITE, linewidths=1.6)
# an off-axis point
p = np.array([2.0, -2.6])
ax.scatter(*p, marker="*", s=260, color="black", zorder=5)
dE = np.sqrt(p @ p); dM = np.sqrt(p @ Sinv @ p)
ax.annotate(f"Euclid {dE:.1f},  Mahalanobis {dM:.1f}", xy=p, xytext=(p[0]+0.6, p[1]-1.6),
            fontsize=9)
ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xlim(-9, 9); ax.set_ylim(-9, 9)
ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
ax.set_title("Grey: Euclidean circles  ·  Red: Mahalanobis ellipses")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"The squared Mahalanobis distance is $x^\top\Sigma^{-1}x$. Where does that exact form show
up in statistics?"* In the **multivariate Gaussian density**, whose exponent is exactly
$-\tfrac12 (x-\mu)^\top\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)$ — a quadratic form in the inverse covariance. The
level sets of constant density *are* the Mahalanobis ellipses, which is why a Gaussian's
contours are ellipses aligned with the covariance eigenvectors. The probabilistic
construction belongs to the Probability track, but the *shape* of those contours is
entirely the content of this article: an ellipsoid whose axes are $\Sigma$'s eigenvectors
and whose radii scale as $\sqrt{\lambda_i}$.
:::

## Example 3 — the edge of applicability: a semidefinite, degenerate form {#sec-degenerate}

Now an honest case where the clean four-shape story strains: a form with a **zero
eigenvalue**. This is not a contrived pathology — it is exactly what happens when data are
*rank-deficient*, e.g. one feature is a perfect linear combination of others (collinearity,
[REGR-07], [GEOM-12]), or when a covariance matrix is estimated from fewer points than
dimensions.

Take the rank-one symmetric matrix

$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix},
\qquad q(x) = x_1^2 - 2 x_1 x_2 + x_2^2 = (x_1 - x_2)^2 .
$$

The form is a *perfect square*, so $q(x) \ge 0$ always — it looks positive — but it is
**zero on an entire line**: any $x$ with $x_1 = x_2$ gives $q = 0$. Its eigenvalues are
$\lambda = 2$ (eigenvector $(1,-1)/\sqrt2$, across which the square grows) and $\lambda = 0$
(eigenvector $(1,1)/\sqrt2$, along which it is flat). This is the **trough** of
@thm-classification, Case 4: positive *semi*definite, not definite. @fig-degenerate shows
the surface — a parabolic valley with a flat floor running off to infinity.

```{python}
#| label: fig-degenerate
#| fig-cap: "The edge case: a positive *semi*definite form $q(x)=(x_1-x_2)^2$, with eigenvalues $2$ and $0$. **Left:** the surface is a trough — a parabola in the $(1,-1)$ direction, but dead flat along the entire $(1,1)$ valley floor (red line), where $q=0$. **Right:** its level sets are not ellipses but parallel straight lines, because the form has no curvature in the flat direction. A 'bowl with a unique bottom' it is not — the minimum is a whole line, the geometric signature of rank deficiency."
A = np.array([[1.0, -1.0], [-1.0, 1.0]])
g = np.linspace(-3, 3, 80)
GX, GY = np.meshgrid(g, g)
Z = (GX - GY)**2

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(9.4, 4.4))
ax1 = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 1, projection="3d")
ax1.plot_surface(GX, GY, Z, cmap="viridis", linewidth=0, antialiased=True, alpha=0.9)
tt = np.linspace(-3, 3, 50)
ax1.plot(tt, tt, np.zeros_like(tt), color=HILITE, lw=3, zorder=10)
ax1.set_title("Trough: flat along $x_1=x_2$", fontsize=11)
ax1.set_xlabel("$x_1$", labelpad=-6); ax1.set_ylabel("$x_2$", labelpad=-6)
ax1.tick_params(labelsize=7, pad=-2); ax1.view_init(elev=24, azim=-120)

ax2 = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 2)
cs = ax2.contour(GX, GY, Z, levels=[0.5, 2, 4.5, 8], colors=GREY, linewidths=1.2)
ax2.clabel(cs, inline=True, fontsize=8, fmt="%.1f")
ax2.plot(tt, tt, color=HILITE, lw=2.4, label="$q=0$ valley floor")
ax2.set_aspect("equal"); ax2.set_xlim(-3, 3); ax2.set_ylim(-3, 3)
ax2.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); ax2.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
ax2.set_title("Level sets are parallel lines, not ellipses")
ax2.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9, loc="upper left")
plt.tight_layout()
```

Why is this the "edge"? Because the comfortable mental model — *quadratic form = ellipse,
unique minimum, invertible matrix* — silently assumes positive **definiteness**. The moment
an eigenvalue hits zero, every one of those conveniences breaks at once: the level sets stop
being ellipses (they become parallel lines), the minimum stops being unique (it is a whole
subspace), the matrix stops being invertible (so $\Sigma^{-1}$ in the Mahalanobis distance
of @sec-mahalanobis *does not exist* — the distance is undefined along the flat direction),
and any optimizer trying to "find the bottom" wanders freely along the trough. The honest
lesson: **a quadratic form is only as well-behaved as its smallest eigenvalue.** When that
eigenvalue is zero (or numerically near zero), you have left the world of bowls and entered
the world of troughs, and you must reach for tools built for it — the pseudoinverse
([GEOM-21]) instead of the inverse, regularization (adding $\lambda I$ to lift the zero
eigenvalue, the ridge trick of [REGR-14]), or a dimension reduction that throws the flat
direction away.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**A nonnegative quadratic form is *not* the same as a positive-definite one.** Both give
$q(x) \ge 0$, but the semidefinite case allows $q(x) = 0$ for some $x \neq 0$ — a flat
direction. The difference is invisible in the inequality "$q \ge 0$" and visible only in
the eigenvalues: definite means all $\lambda_i > 0$ (strictly), semidefinite allows
$\lambda_i = 0$. Conflating them is how people end up inverting a singular matrix, trusting
a "minimum" that is actually a ridge, or computing a Mahalanobis distance that is secretly
infinite in some direction. The strict-vs-nonstrict distinction is the entire subject of
[GEOM-18].
:::

# When the quadratic form is the right lens — and when it is not {#sec-applicability}

The quadratic form is the correct tool whenever the quantity you care about is genuinely
**second order and built from a symmetric matrix** — variance, squared distance,
least-squares error, local curvature, energy. In those settings the eigen-picture is
complete and exact: you know the shape (signs), the axes (eigenvectors), and the
steepnesses (eigenvalues), and @thm-rayleigh hands you the extremes for free. This is why
the form is the unifying object of Movement V and recurs throughout the series.

It is the *wrong* lens in three honest situations, and a graduate-level answer names them:

- **When the matrix is not symmetric and the antisymmetric part carries meaning.** The form
  $x^\top A x$ throws the antisymmetric part of $A$ away (@lem-symmetrize). If your $A$
  encodes a rotation or a flow where the skew part matters (e.g. the curl of a vector field,
  a circulation), the quadratic form is blind to it — you need the full matrix action
  $x \mapsto Ax$ ([GEOM-08]), not the scalar $x^\top A x$.
- **When the surface is genuinely non-quadratic far from a point.** A quadratic form is the
  *exact* shape only for actually-quadratic quantities, and the *local* shape (via the
  second-order Taylor expansion, [GEOM-24]) for everything else. Far from the expansion
  point, a real loss surface has higher-order structure — multiple basins, plateaus, cliffs
  — that no single quadratic form captures. Trusting the quadratic model globally is how
  Newton's method oversteps; it is a *local* picture.
- **When the matrix is (near-)singular.** As @sec-degenerate showed, the clean story needs
  $\lambda_{\min} > 0$. With a zero or tiny smallest eigenvalue you are in the
  semidefinite/ill-conditioned regime, and you reach for the pseudoinverse, regularization,
  or a low-rank truncation instead of the naive inverse.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[GEOM-16 *Symmetric Matrices and the Spectral Theorem*]**, which proved
that a symmetric $A$ factors as $Q\Lambda Q^\top$ with real eigenvalues and an orthonormal
eigenbasis. This article spent that factorization: it is the entire reason a quadratic form
collapses to $\sum_i \lambda_i z_i^2$, and hence the entire reason there are only four
shapes. From here Movement V continues:

- **[GEOM-18 *Positive-Definite Matrices*]** — Case 1 of @thm-classification, studied in
  full: the "good" matrices whose form is a bowl with a unique bottom, the tests for them
  (eigenvalues, leading minors, Cholesky), and why $A^\top A$ and covariance matrices are
  the canonical examples. This article supplied the *picture*; [GEOM-18] supplies the
  *theory and the tests*.
- **[GEOM-19 *Cholesky and the Matrix Square Root*]** — once a form is positive definite,
  it factors as a "square," powering whitening and Gaussian sampling.
- **[GEOM-25 *The Gradient and Hessian of a Quadratic Form*]** — differentiates
  $f(x) = \tfrac12 x^\top A x - b^\top x$, finds $\nabla f = Ax - b$ and $\nabla^2 f = A$,
  and shows that minimizing a quadratic form *is* solving $Ax = b$ — the convergence point
  of the whole track, discharging Regression's and Inference's heaviest requests.

Downstream of the track, this article is the algebra behind **variance maximization in PCA**
(Unsupervised Learning, via @thm-rayleigh), the **Fisher-information quadratic form**
([INFR-B3]), and the **quadratic model of a loss** that Optimization minimizes.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary thread.** J. J. Sylvester's 1852 paper introducing the law of inertia
  (@thm-inertia) is the origin of "signature"; the principal-axis idea traces back to Euler
  and Lagrange's classification of conics and quadrics, which is the two- and
  three-dimensional special case of @thm-principal-axes.
- **The Rayleigh quotient** (@sec-rayleigh) is named for Lord Rayleigh's work on vibrational
  modes; the same variational characterization of eigenvalues — that
  $\lambda_{\max} = \max_{\|u\|=1} u^\top A u$ — generalizes to the **Courant–Fischer
  min–max theorem** for the interior eigenvalues, a standard graduate result and the
  rigorous foundation under PCA's "the $k$-th component maximizes residual variance."
- **Open threads deliberately deferred.** *Exactly when is the form a bowl with a unique
  bottom, and how do I test it without computing eigenvalues?* — positive-definiteness and
  Sylvester's minor criterion, [GEOM-18]. *What if I want to differentiate the form?* —
  [GEOM-25]. *What happens to the form when the matrix is rectangular, so it has no
  eigenvalues?* — there is no quadratic form for a rectangular matrix, but the analogous
  "stretch along orthogonal axes" picture survives as the **singular value decomposition**,
  [GEOM-20], where $A^\top A$ (a quadratic-form matrix) is exactly the bridge.
